coredata.dc:title: In-situ reduction and deposition of Ag nanoparticles on black phosphorus nanosheets co-loaded with graphene oxide as a broad spectrum photocatalyst for enhanced photocatalytic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xin
Zhou, Benqing
Zhang, Youming
Liu, Liwei
Song, Jun
Hu, Rui
Qu, Junle
coredata.dc:description: A novel ternary nanohybrid comprising Ag nanoparticles anchored on black phosphorus nanosheets (BPNS) supported on the surface of graphene oxide (GO) has been synthesized for the first time. UV–Vis spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy results revealed that BPNS with highly active P atoms on its surface served as a strong reductant to reduce Ag precursor and the emerging Ag atoms were consequently deposited on its surface. The Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid showed excellent photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of methylene blue under solar light irradiation. Broad spectrum response of BPNS, effective trapping of the electrons of Ag nanoparticles and high stability of Ag-BPNS on GO surface contributed the enhanced photocatalytic performance. A plausible mechanism has been proposed to explain the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid
Ag nanoparticles
Black phosphorus nanosheets
Methylene blue

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61705143 / 61235012 / 61378091 / 61525503 / 61620106016 ); the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2018M630979 / 2017M622766 / 2017M622748 ); the National Basic Research Program of China ( 2015CB352005 ); Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan cooperation innovation platform & major projects of international cooperation in Colleges and Universities in Guangdong Province ( 2015KGJHZ002 ); and the Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen ( KQJSCX20170327151457055 / JCYJ20150930104948169 / JCYJ20160328144746940 / GJHZ20160226202139185 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM image of the as-obtained BPNS. (b) Height profiles along the white lines shown in (a).

(a) Time-dependent UV–Vis absorption spectra of the reduction of silver–dodecylamine complexes in the presence of BPNS. The inset shows the plot of Amax against the reaction time. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of BPNS, Ag-BPNS, GO, and Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid. The inset shows the photographs of BPNS and the resultant Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid. HAADF-STEM image of dark-field (c) and corresponding EDX elemental mapping of P (d), C (e), and Ag (f) of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid.
(a) Raman spectra of GO, BP, BPNS, Ag-BPNS, and Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid. (b) Magnified Raman spectra of GO and Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid in (a).
(a) Full scan XPS spectrum of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid. High-resolution XPS spectra of Ag 3d (b), C1s (c), O1s (d), bulk P2p (e), and BPNS P2p (f) regions of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid.
TGA curves of GO and Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid.
Comparison of photocatalytic activity of GO, BPNS, Ag-BPNS, and Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid for the degradation of MB solution compared to a blank run (no catalyst).

(a) Plots of –ln[(C t)/(C 0)] against the reaction time in the cases of GO, BPNS, Ag-BPNS, and Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid used as photocatalysts, compared with a blank run (no catalyst). (b) The corresponding rate constants (k) obtained in a.
Plots of (C t)/(C 0) against the irradiation time using Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid as the photocatalyst in three successive runs and then tested twice again after a week.
The rate constants (k) of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid and Ag-BPNS in three successive runs and then tested twice again after a week.

(a) Photocurrent transient responses of GO, BPNS, and Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid under >420 nm solar light irradiation. (b) ESR spectra of the DMPO-O2·– adducts recorded with Ag-BPNS/GO hybrids under dark and >420 nm visible light irradiation for 5 min and 10 min, respectively.
Schematic diagram illustrating the transfer of photogenerated charges of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid under Xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm) irradiation and the possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid.

head.ce:title: In-situ reduction and deposition of Ag nanoparticles on black phosphorus nanosheets co-loaded with graphene oxide as a broad spectrum photocatalyst for enhanced photocatalytic performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biophotonics Research Laboratory, Center of Interdisciplinary Biomedical Education and Research, College of Mathematics and Science, University of Central Oklahoma, Oklahoma, 73034, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Interdisciplinary Biomedical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Hong Kong, 999077, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ternary nanohybrid comprising Ag nanoparticles anchored on black phosphorus nanosheets (BPNS) supported on the surface of graphene oxide (GO) has been synthesized for the first time. UV–Vis spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy results revealed that BPNS with highly active P atoms on its surface served as a strong reductant to reduce Ag precursor and the emerging Ag atoms were consequently deposited on its surface. The Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid showed excellent photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of methylene blue under solar light irradiation. Broad spectrum response of BPNS, effective trapping of the electrons of Ag nanoparticles and high stability of Ag-BPNS on GO surface contributed the enhanced photocatalytic performance. A plausible mechanism has been proposed to explain the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid.

ce:section.ce:para: As a result of rapid industrialization and population growth, large amounts of poisonous and hazardous pollutants are being produced and released into the environment, which has become a serious problem in many countries [1–3]. In order to degrade toxic and hazardous chemicals in different wastewaters, the development of new technologies has garnered a lot of interest. Among these methods, photocatalysis is regarded as one of the most promising techniques for solving the environmental pollution issues [4–8]. Utilizing solar light to degrade harmful and toxic dyes through photocatalysis has been investigated extensively because solar energy, as a free, clean, green, and inexhaustible resource, is a promising renewable source of energy [9,10]. From the viewpoint of efficient utilization of solar energy, the exploitation of visible light and the near-infrared (NIR) light-active photocatalysts is essential for practical applications. This is because visible and NIR light occupies approximately 95% of the solar light energy spectrum [11,12]. Although various photocatalysts such as oxides [13,14], (oxy) nitrides [15,16], elemental photocatalysts [17,18], and plasmonic photocatalysts [19,20] have been prepared in the past few decades, the search and optimization of highly efficient photocatalysts that are capable of harvesting the broad range of solar spectrum still remains a challenge.

Recently, black phosphorus (BP), with its high charge-carrier mobility, tunable direct bandgap, and unique in-plane anisotropic structure, is becoming a rising star of post-graphene two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials and has been intensively investigated for the fabrication of sensors since the first report on field-effect transistors in 2014 [21,22]. As the direct bandgap of BP can be adjusted from ∼0.3 eV (bulk BP) to ∼2.1 eV (BP monolayer), 2D BP is expected to be a promising visible and NIR light-activated functional material in the application of optical and photocatalysis [23,24]. Recently, Tian et al. demonstrated the BP nanosheets could be utilized as a water-splitting photocatalyst for H2 evolution with a high energy conversion efficiency [25]. Majima and coworkers have also reported that BP nanosheets can be used as a near-infrared-activated photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution [26]. Inspired from these studies, it is reasonable to fabricate a nanohybrid based on BP nanosheets for photocatalytic pollutant degradation. In fact, photocatalysts with relatively high productivity in pollutant degradation have not yet been found.

Herein, we report a synthetic method to synthesize a novel ternary nanohybrid comprising Ag nanoparticles anchored on black phosphorus nanosheets (BPNS) supported on graphene oxide (GO). The fabricated nanohybrid was characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), the high-angle annular dark field-scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM), UV–Vis spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, Thermal analysis (TGA), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The photocatalytic activities for the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under solar light irradiation over the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid, Ag-BPNS, BPNS, and GO were comparatively investigated. The possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity was proposed based on the results of photocurrent responses and the electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra.

The BP crystals were purchased from XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd and stored in a dark Ar glovebox. Graphene oxide (4 mg/mL, dispersion in water), 1-Dodecylamin (99%) and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) (99.5%) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), methylene blue (MB) and ethanol were all obtained from Aladdin Reagents. All other chemicals used in this study were analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

The BPNS were obtained using an NMP solvent exfoliation process involving probe sonication followed by bath sonication of bulk BP powders. Typically, 50 mg of BP crystals was added into 50 mL of NMP in an 80 mL sealed conical tube and sonicated with probe sonication for 2 h at the power of 1200 W. The ultrasonic frequency was from 19 to 25 kHz and the ultrasound probe worked 2 s with the interval of 4 s. Then the dispersion was sonicated in an ultrasonic bath continuously for another 10 h at the power of 300 W. The resulting dispersion was centrifuged for 20 min at 5000 rpm to remove the bulk BP (about 30 mg), and the supernatant containing BPNS was decanted gently. Then, the dispersion containing BPNS was centrifuged for 20 min at 11000 rpm, the precipitate product was re-suspended in 10 mL NMP solution (about 2 mg/mL) for further use (Scheme 1 (a)).

In the synthesis of Ag-BPNS, a stock solution of Ag precursor (silver-dodecylamine complexes, 5 mg/mL Ag) was prepared by adding 170 mg AgNO3 and 0.5 g 1-Dodecylamin to 20 mL ethanol under stirring. Then, 2 mL of silver-dodecylamine complexes solution was added to 10 mL NMP solution containing BPNS. The reduction was immediately initiated as the BPNS can serve as a strong reductant. During the reduction, 0.25 mL of the sample solution was taken and diluted into 3 mL NMP for UV–vis spectroscopy characterization. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm/min for 10 min and washed three times with ethanol, and the solid product was re-dispersed in 10 mL ethanol for further use (about 3 mg/mL).

In a typical synthesis of a ternary Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid, 3 mL of Ag-BPNS dispersion (3 mg/mL) was slowly added to 8 mL ethanol containing 8 mg GO (4 mg/mL, 2 mL) under sonication. Then, the dispersion was sonicated for another 30 min and the product solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm/min for 10 min and the solid product was washed three times with ethanol and water, respectively, and then re-dispersed in 10 mL water for further use (Scheme 1(b)).

UV–vis spectra were recorded in the range from 200 to 800 nm using a Shimadzu UV-1780 spectrometer. TEM images were recorded on a Philips Tecnai G2 Spirit microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV, HRTEM image was obtained using a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 Spirit microscope operated at 300 kV accelerating voltage, HAADF-STEM image was obtained with a Titan Cubed Themis G2 300 microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV equipped with a spherical aberration corrector, and the samples were prepared by dropping a diluted dispersion of the sample onto a 200 mesh carbon-coated copper grid. XPS measurements were made on a VG ESCALAB MK II spectrometer using an Al Kα X-ray source (hν = 1486.6 eV). The X-ray source was operated at 12.5 kV and 250 W. AFM measurements were performed using a Veeco DI Nanoscope Multi Mode V system. Raman scattering was performed on a Horiba Jobin-Yvon Lab Ram HR VIS high-resolution confocal Raman microscope equipped with a 633 nm laser as the excitation source at room temperature. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were conducted on a Pyris Diamond TG/DTA (PerkinElmer) apparatus at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 (40–800 °C in O2/N2 flow). Photocurrent measurements were performed on a M304979 electrochemical workstation using a three-electrode cell with the nanostructure materials on FTO as the working electrode, saturated calomel electrode and platinum electrode as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals were examined on a Bruker ER200-SRC-10/12 spectrometer under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm).

The photocatalytic activity of the BP-Ag/TiO2 hybrid was evaluated towards the degradation of MB at room temperature under ambient conditions. For the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid, 5 mL of MB (1 × 10−3 M) was placed into 45 mL of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid supernatant (1 mg/mL). Subsequently, the solution was sonicated for 10 min and magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 min to ensure the complete adsorption-desorption equilibrium of MB on the surface of the photocatalyst. A 300 W xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm long-pass filter was used. The light intensity in the center of the beaker was measured to be 300 mW/cm2 using a Newport optical power/energy meter (842-PE). During the illumination, the reaction temperature was maintained at room temperature. At certain time intervals, ∼0.5 mL of the suspension was collected and centrifuged at 14800 rpm for 3 min to remove the catalyst. The photoactivity was monitored by measuring the solution absorbance at the maximum wavelength of 660 nm (MB) using a Shimadzu UV-1780 spectrophotometer. For the successive MB degradation reactions, when one round of the reaction was finished, another 5 mL of MB dye was added to the reaction solution for a next reaction cycle. This reaction was repeated three times to study the catalytic stability of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid.

BPNS was obtained by a NMP solvent exfoliation method as illustrated in Scheme 1 [27], where the BP crystals were first exfoliated by probe sonication to form large-sized BPNS (typically ∼2 μm) in size, as confirmed by the TEM image in Fig. 1 (a). Subsequently, BPNS were further exfoliated by bath sonication to obtain smaller-sized BPNS as illustrated in Fig. 1(b). The thickness and size of BPNS were further confirmed by atomic force microscopy (AFM). Fig. 1(c) shows a characteristic topographic image of BPNS after bath sonication, in which the size of the generated BPNS is ∼40–60 nm and the thickness of BPNS is ∼3–5 nm (Fig. 1(d)).

BPNS with exposed highly active P atoms on its surface can serve as a strong reductant in the synthesis of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid. In this case, a UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to study the reduction of the Ag precursor to Ag nanoparticles and their deposition on BP surfaces. Fig. 2 (a) shows the time-dependent UV–Vis absorption spectra of the reduction of silver–dodecylamine complexes in the presence of BPNS. The addition of BPNS into the solution of silver–dodecylamine complexes led to the quick reduction (<1 min) of the complexes, as evident from the rapid increase in the intensity of the absorption peak at ∼430 nm [28]. After a minute, the absorbance almost ceased to increase, indicating complete reduction. Meanwhile, the absorbance gradually red-shifted towards higher wavelength, indicating the complete reduction of Ag precursor and growth of Ag nanoparticles [29]. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer was also employed to analyze the optical properties of Ag-BPNS and Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid. Compared with GO and BPNS, Ag-BPNS showed a strong absorption peak at 430 nm in the visible region, with a tail extending into the long wavelength region, as indicated in Fig. 2(b). Moreover, as Ag-BPNS was dispersed on GO to form the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid, the intensity of the absorption peak increased further and the spectral shape became close to the overlap of the peaks of Ag-BPNS and GO, which indicated that the resultant Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid would have a good ability to harvest solar light [30].

The morphology and microstructure of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid materials were characterized by TEM and HRTEM. It was evident from these analyses that the synthesized Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid material was highly dispersed, and no large and irregular Ag particles could be seen in the TEM image of Fig. 3 (a), indicating that BPNS could stabilize and maintain the small Ag nanoparticles formed on its surfaces [29]. The corresponding HRTEM image in Fig. 3(b) displays the round shape of Ag nanoparticles with an interplanar spacing of 0.223 nm, which corresponded to the (1,-1,2) lattice planes of Ag. Furthermore, HAADF-STEM was used to discriminate Ag nanoparticles deposited on the surface of BPNS. As Ag atoms are heavier than P and O atoms in the Ag/BPNS, Ag nanoparticles were brighter in the dark-field of the HAADF-STEM image. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the Ag nanoparticles were well-dispersed on the surface of BPNS. The elemental mappings of P, C, and Ag elements gave clearer images of the high dispersion in Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid in Fig. 3(d, e and f), indicating a successful synthesis of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid.

Raman spectroscopy is an effective technique for sample identification as it provides detailed information about the vibrational and rotational modes [31]. Herein, Raman spectra were measured to study the structural transformation of BP during the liquid exfoliation process. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the Raman spectra of bulk BP and exfoliated nanosheets showed nearly identical peaks located at ∼361, 438, and 466 cm−1, which could be assigned to the Ag 1, B2g, and Ag 2 modes of BP, respectively. This result indicated that the exfoliated BP nanosheets retained the structure of their bulk counterpart [32]. Compared to bulk BP, the Raman spectrum of ultrathin BP nanosheets showed a slight shift toward high wavenumbers, which could be ascribed to the ultrathin thickness of the nanosheets as discussed in previous reports [33]. No peaks could be observed unless magnified as shown in Fig. 4(b), because of the low weight ratio of BP in the composites and highly dispersed BP on the GO surface.

XPS spectra were also measured to investigate the surface composition of the as-obtained Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), Ag, P, C, and O were all detected, confirming the presence of Ag and BP on the surface of GO. Moreover, the Ag nanoparticles on the BPNS surface were mainly in the form of metallic Ag (Ag 3d peak at 367.7 eV and 373.7 eV) as shown in Fig. 5(b) [29]. From the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s in Fig. 5(c), the signals at 284.7 and 287.1 eV demonstrated the presence of CC, COH and OCO respectively [34,35]. The O 1s peaks at binding energies of 530.8, 531.6, 532.8, and 533.5 eV were assigned to PO, POP, and POH moieties, and oxygenated carbon, respectively (Fig. 5(d)). The high-resolution P 2p XPS spectra of BPNS before and after reduction of the Ag precursor are illustrated in Fig. 5(e) and (f). A sharp peak at 129.7 and a shoulder peak at 130.5 eV, as well as a broad peak in the region of 132.0–136.0 eV could be observed (Fig. 5(e)). Fitting analysis showed that the P 2p peaks of BPNS could be deconvoluted into five peaks at 129.7, 130.5, 131.6, 132.8, and 134.3 eV. The peaks at 129.7 and 130.5 were assigned to P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 of the PP bonds, which are characteristic of crystalline BP. Also, the peaks at 131.6, 132.8, and 134.3 eV were ascribed to POP (bridging bonding), OPO (dangling bonding), and P2O5, which originated from oxygen defects or surface suboxides on the BP surfaces [36,37]. After the reduction of the Ag precursor, only the peaks corresponding to the oxidized phosphorus in BPNS were observed as the peaks of PP bonds had almost disappeared. This indicated that BPNS acted as a reducing agent and was oxidized during the reduction of the Ag precursor [33]. Fig. 6 shows the curves of GO and Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid, we can find that the content of Ag-BPNS was ∼40%. Furthermore, the dispersion of Ag-BPNS on GO significantly enhanced the thermal stability of the nanohybrid.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-obtained Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid was evaluated by performing the degradation of MB dye as a model reaction. Typically, an aqueous solution of MB (50 mL, 1 × 10−4 M) mixed with the photocatalyst (about 50 mg) was irradiated with solar light using a 300 W Xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm long-pass filter. Fig. 7 displays the photocatalytic activities toward the degradation of MB solution over Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid samples, in which the normalized absorption maximum of MB (Ct/C0) was plotted against the reaction time, where Ct and C0 correspond to the concentrations of the MB solution at time t and 0, respectively [38]. As a comparison, GO, BPNS, and Ag-BPNS with comparable Ag loading were also synthesized and used in this study. It could be seen that the photocatalytic activities were very low in the absence of the photocatalysts and over GO under visible light irradiation for 90 min. BPNS exhibited the weakest photocatalytic activity with a degradation percentage of 20%, which was attributed to the rapid recombination of the electrons and holes, as no electron mediator was present to store and shuttle the photogenerated electrons [36]. Both Ag-BPNS and the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid exhibited greatly enhanced the photocatalytic activities, as the photocatalytic degradation percentages were ∼79% and 94%, respectively, after 90 min of visible light irradiation. It was evident that the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid showed the best photocatalytic activity among these samples. The high photocatalytic activity of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid could be attributed to the complete harvesting of visible light, the effective separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, as well as the high dispersion and stability of Ag-BPNS on the GO surface.

The kinetics of photocatalytic degradation reaction were that of a pseudo-first order reaction, and the apparent rate constants (k) of different samples were estimated by the slopes of the fitting lines presented in Fig. 8 (a) [39]. The apparent rate constant of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid was ∼0.0313 min−1, which is ∼2.2 and 11.7 times higher than those of Ag-BPNS (k = 0.0141 min−1) and BPNS (k = 0.00267 min−1), respectively (Fig. 8(b)). Successive photocatalytic experiments for the degradation of MB solution were also performed to evaluate the stability of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid. As shown in Fig. 9 , the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid was still ∼96% after three successive catalysis runs. Furthermore, the separated photocatalysts were tested again after one week, and the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid reached almost ∼81%. The slight decrease of activity can be ascribed to the oxidation of BPNS since the high active P atoms exposed on BPNS surface are sensitive to O2. The corresponding rate constant of Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid was almost constant in three successive runs and still maintained at ∼0.0180 min−1 after tested twice again one week later. However, Ag-BPNS without GO dramatically lost its catalytic performance and exhibited almost no catalytic performance after one week, indicated that GO can effective stabilize Ag-BPNS against aggregation during the degradation (Fig. 10 ).

An efficient separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and transfer over the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid were likely responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the transient photocurrent responses were measured to evaluate the interfacial charge separation efficiency. Typically, the photocurrent is generated by the transfer of photogenerated electrons to the counter electrode, and a higher photocurrent indicates a more efficient charge separation and thus a longer lifetime of the photogenerated electrons [40]. Fig. 11 (a) shows the photocurrent response of GO, BPNS, and the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid when irradiated with >420 nm solar light. The photocurrent of the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid was found to be ∼1.5 and 2.5 times larger than that of GO and BPNS, respectively, and also exhibited good repeatability in the on/off cycles. This result indicated that the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid exhibited a higher electron-hole separation efficiency in comparison to GO and BPNS. Additionally, the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique with DMPO was also carried out to examine the reactive species during the photocatalytic process [41]. As illustrated in Fig. 11(b), the characteristic signals of DMPO-·O2 − could be observed for the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid, implying that the ·O2 − species was generated over the nanohybrid under visible light irradiation. In this case, the active species in the photocatalytic reaction are ·O2 − and h+, and the corresponding photocatalytic mechanism is shown in Fig. 12 . Upon solar light irradiation, efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and transfer takes place over the Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid At the same time, Ag nanoparticles act as a cocatalyst for the rapid transfer of the photogenerated electrons and then reduce O2 to ·O2 − via an ultrafast one electron process [41]. The holes (h+) in the valence band of BPNS as well as the ·O2 − species can then effectively degrade the pollutant. Moreover, GO allows electron transfer and also effectively stabilizes Ag-BPNS as a highly disperse hybrid on its surface.

In summary, a ternary Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid was successfully synthesized for the first time. By the action of a strong reducing agent, BPNS, the silver–dodecylamine complex was reduced and Ag atoms were deposited on its surface. The obtained Ag-BPNS/GO nanohybrid showed excellent photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of MB under solar light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). This enhanced photocatalytic performance was attributed to the full harvesting of visible light, the effective separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, as well as the high dispersion and stability of Ag-BPNS on the GO surface.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61705143/61235012/61378091/61525503/61620106016); the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M630979/2017M622766/2017M622748); the National Basic Research Program of China (2015CB352005); Hong Kong, Macao, and Taiwan cooperation innovation platform & major projects of international cooperation in Colleges and Universities in Guangdong Province (2015KGJHZ002); and the Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen (KQJSCX20170327151457055/JCYJ20150930104948169/JCYJ20160328144746940/GJHZ20160226202139185).

Supported black phosphorus nanosheets as hydrogen-evolving photocatalyst achieving 5.4% energy conversion efficiency at 353 K
